items.question: For Q1 = 25 and Q3 = 35, which values are flagged by Tukey's 1.5×IQR rule?
A feature is strongly right-skewed. Which detection method is usually the safer default?
items.explanation: IQR uses quartiles and tolerates skew; mean and std in z-scores are pulled by the tail and mis-rank typical points.

items.question: Why should clip bounds be computed on the training set only?
options: To prevent leakage from validation/test distributions into preprocessing
Because test sets never have outliers
Because clipping is only for inference
items.answer: To prevent leakage from validation/test distributions into preprocessing
items.explanation: Holdout statistics must not influence train-time preprocessing; otherwise offline metrics are optimistically biased.

items.question: Which treatment keeps all rows but limits the influence of extreme values?
items.explanation: Clipping caps values at train-fitted bounds without deleting observations, reducing leverage while preserving sample size.

items.question: In fraud detection, why is blanket removal of IQR outliers often wrong?
options: IQR cannot be computed on integers
Fraudulent transactions are often statistically extreme—the signal lives in the tail
Fraud data is always normally distributed
items.answer: Fraudulent transactions are often statistically extreme—the signal lives in the tail
items.explanation: Rare high-risk events look like outliers by construction; deleting them removes positives the model must learn.
